_meta.pageSubtitle: Plain-English definitions of the CRA, security, and SBOM vocabulary you'll encounter across Seentrix.

vulnerability.title: Vulnerability
vulnerability.body: A weakness in a product's code, design, or configuration that an attacker can exploit to affect confidentiality, integrity, or availability. A vulnerability is different from a bug: all exploitable bugs are vulnerabilities, but not all bugs have security impact. In Seentrix, vulnerabilities are detected by scanning the active SBOM and by direct reports through the PSIRT intake.

cve.title: CVE
cve.body: Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures — a public identifier (e.g. CVE-2024-12345) assigned by MITRE to each publicly-known software vulnerability. CVEs are the lingua franca for vulnerability tracking: vendors, scanners, and advisories all reference them. Seentrix attaches the CVE id to every finding so your audit trail is interoperable with any external tool.
cve.ref: CRA Article 13(8) implicitly references CVEs when it requires manufacturers to "address vulnerabilities" — auditors expect your fix tickets to cite CVE ids.

cvss.title: CVSS
cvss.body: Common Vulnerability Scoring System — a 0.0 to 10.0 score quantifying how severe a vulnerability is. Scores break into Low (0.1-3.9), Medium (4.0-6.9), High (7.0-8.9), Critical (9.0-10.0). CVSS v4 (2023) is the current version; many scanners still report v3. Use CVSS as a starting point for triage, not the final decision — EPSS and KEV often change the priority.

epss.title: EPSS
epss.body: Exploit Prediction Scoring System — a 0.0 to 1.0 probability estimate, updated daily, that a given CVE will be exploited in the next 30 days. Most CVEs have an EPSS below 0.01. An EPSS above 0.5 combined with a high CVSS usually means "patch this week." EPSS is maintained by the FIRST.org community.

kev.title: KEV catalog
kev.body: CISA's Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog — a curated list of CVEs that are actively being exploited in the wild. If a vulnerability in your SBOM is on the KEV list, it should jump to the top of your triage queue regardless of its CVSS. CISA adds ~5-10 CVEs to the KEV catalog per week.

actively_exploited.title: Actively exploited
actively_exploited.body: A vulnerability is actively exploited when there's credible evidence of real-world attacks using it — public proof-of-concept weaponised, threat-intel reports, log evidence, or presence on the CISA KEV catalog. In Seentrix you can flag any vulnerability as actively-exploited, which immediately triggers the Article 14 incident reporting clock.
actively_exploited.ref: CRA Article 14(1) — manufacturers must notify ENISA of any actively-exploited vulnerability in their products within 24 hours of becoming aware.

advisory.title: Security advisory
advisory.body: A formal, public statement describing a vulnerability, its impact, and the fix. Usually published in machine-readable formats like CSAF or VEX so downstream users can automatically ingest it. Publishing advisories is one of the core obligations of running a PSIRT.

cvd.title: Coordinated Vulnerability Disclosure
cvd.body: A policy + process for handling incoming vulnerability reports: a public intake, a triage SLA, a safe-harbour statement for good-faith researchers, and coordinated timing between the vendor's patch and the researcher's public disclosure. The CRA makes CVD a formal obligation.
cvd.ref: CRA Annex I Part II(1) — manufacturers must have a CVD policy. Seentrix's public security page is the intake side of that policy.

psirt.title: PSIRT
psirt.body: Product Security Incident Response Team — the team (not necessarily full-time) that owns vulnerability intake, triage, coordination with researchers, and publication of advisories for a product. For small companies the PSIRT might be one engineer wearing a second hat. The CRA assumes you have one even if you don't call it that.

sbom.title: SBOM
sbom.body: Software Bill of Materials — a machine-readable inventory of every component (including transitive dependencies) in a piece of software, along with versions and licences. The SBOM is the input to vulnerability scanning: you can't find a vulnerability if you don't know what you ship. Seentrix accepts CycloneDX and SPDX.
sbom.ref: CRA Annex I Part I(2)(a)(v) — products must "identify and document relevant components." An SBOM is the practical way to satisfy that requirement.

cyclonedx.title: CycloneDX
cyclonedx.body: An SBOM format maintained by OWASP (XML or JSON). CycloneDX is popular in the security-tooling ecosystem and is particularly good at representing dependency relationships and vulnerabilities alongside components. Seentrix scans CycloneDX natively.

spdx.title: SPDX
spdx.body: Software Package Data Exchange — an ISO/IEC 5962 standardised SBOM format maintained by the Linux Foundation (tag-value, JSON, YAML, RDF). SPDX has the strongest licence-representation capabilities of any SBOM format and is the reference format for US federal procurement. Seentrix scans SPDX natively.

semver.title: Semver
semver.body: Semantic Versioning — a three-number versioning convention (MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH) where bumps have defined meanings: MAJOR for breaking changes, MINOR for backwards-compatible features, PATCH for backwards-compatible fixes. Seentrix accepts any version string, but semver-compliant versions make it easier for users to decide whether to upgrade.

digest.title: Digest
digest.body: A fixed-length fingerprint (usually a SHA-256 hash) of a file or artifact. If you change one byte, the digest changes completely. Digests let downstream users verify that the binary they downloaded is the one you shipped — essential for supply-chain integrity.

hash.title: Hash
hash.body: A one-way mathematical function that maps data of any size to a fixed-length output (the digest). Cryptographic hashes (SHA-256, SHA-3) are designed so that different inputs produce different outputs and so you can't reverse the output back to the input. Used for integrity checks and signatures.

signing.title: Code signing
signing.body: Attaching a cryptographic signature to a software artifact to prove it came from you and hasn't been tampered with. Implementations vary: GPG for open-source, Authenticode for Windows, Sigstore for cloud-native, OS-vendor signing for mobile. Signed releases are a practical way to satisfy the CRA's integrity-protection requirements.
signing.ref: CRA Annex I Part I(3)(h) — products must implement integrity protection for stored, transmitted, and processed data.

cra.title: CRA
cra.body: Cyber Resilience Act — Regulation (EU) 2024/2847. An EU regulation that imposes cybersecurity requirements on "products with digital elements" placed on the EU market, including hardware, software, firmware, and IoT. Adopted 2024, with obligations phasing in through 2027 (full compliance by 11 December 2027).

annex_i.title: Annex I
annex_i.body: The essential cybersecurity requirements, split into Part I (cybersecurity requirements for the product itself — secure-by-default, integrity, data minimisation, etc.) and Part II (vulnerability-handling requirements — SBOM, CVD policy, security updates, etc.). Every product in scope must meet every applicable requirement.

annex_v.title: Annex V
annex_v.body: The mandatory content of the EU Declaration of Conformity: manufacturer identification, product identification, list of harmonised standards applied, conformity-assessment route, notified-body details (where relevant), signature of an authorised person. Seentrix populates all of this automatically from the settings you provide.

article_13.title: Article 13
article_13.body: The CRA article that enumerates the manufacturer's obligations: CE marking, technical documentation, conformity assessment, vulnerability handling, support period, contact point for cybersecurity, and the duty to provide security updates without undue delay for the duration of the support period (minimum 5 years).

article_14.title: Article 14
article_14.body: The CRA article that mandates incident reporting. When a manufacturer becomes aware of an actively-exploited vulnerability or a severe incident affecting their product's security, they must file an Early Warning within 24 hours, a full Incident Report within 72 hours, and a Final Report within 14 days — all via the ENISA single entry point.

essential_requirements.title: Essential requirements
essential_requirements.body: The list of cybersecurity properties a product must have to be placed on the EU market, defined in CRA Annex I Part I. Examples: no known exploitable vulnerabilities at the time of placing on the market, secure-by-default configuration, minimised attack surface, protection of integrity and authenticity, data minimisation. Compliance with harmonised standards (EN 18031 series) gives you a presumption of conformity.

conformity_assessment.title: Conformity assessment
conformity_assessment.body: The formal procedure that determines whether your product meets the essential requirements. The CRA offers four routes: Module A (self-assessment, default for non-critical), Module B+C (type examination by a notified body for Important Class II), Module H (full quality-assurance audit), and the EU cybersecurity certification scheme.

module_a.title: Module A
module_a.body: Internal production control — the self-assessment route. The manufacturer prepares the technical documentation, declares conformity, affixes the CE marking, and assumes full responsibility. No notified body is involved. Available for products that aren't classified Important Class I or II or Critical.

module_b_c.title: Module B+C
module_b_c.body: EU-type examination (Module B) followed by conformity to type (Module C). A notified body examines a representative sample of the product, issues an EU-type examination certificate, and then the manufacturer declares every produced unit conforms to that type. Required for Important Class II products.

module_h.title: Module H
module_h.body: Full quality assurance — a notified body audits and approves the manufacturer's complete quality system (design, manufacturing, final inspection, testing). Surveillance audits recur annually. More expensive than Module B+C but covers all product variants under one certificate. Required for Critical products and available for Important Class II.

ce_marking.title: CE marking
ce_marking.body: The CE logo on products and packaging, signalling compliance with applicable EU regulations. Under the CRA, affixing CE marking to a product with digital elements is an attestation that it meets the essential requirements and has been through the correct conformity-assessment route. Illegitimate CE marking carries fines up to €15M or 2.5% of global turnover.

doc.title: Declaration of Conformity
doc.body: A signed legal document where the manufacturer declares their product complies with every applicable EU regulation (CRA + others). Must be drawn up before placing the product on the market, kept on file for 10 years, and made available to market surveillance authorities on request. Seentrix generates it from your product + organisation settings.
doc.ref: CRA Annex V defines the mandatory content of the Declaration of Conformity.

notified_body.title: Notified body
notified_body.body: An independent third-party organisation designated by an EU member state to perform conformity assessments. Each has a unique four-digit identifier (e.g. 0197 for TÜV Rheinland) and a scope of activities they're competent to assess. You must pick one before applying for Module B+C or Module H.

nando.title: NANDO
nando.body: The Commission's New Approach Notified and Designated Organisations database. Every notified body competent to perform CRA conformity assessments is listed there with their scope, address, and four-digit ID. The authoritative place to pick a notified body. Search: ec.europa.eu/growth/tools-databases/nando.

authorised_representative.title: Authorised representative
authorised_representative.body: A natural or legal person established in the EU, mandated in writing by a non-EU manufacturer, to carry out specific tasks on the manufacturer's behalf (keeping technical documentation, cooperating with market surveillance, etc.). Required before a non-EU manufacturer can place products on the EU market.

market_surveillance.title: Market surveillance
market_surveillance.body: The national-authority function that ensures products placed on the EU market comply with applicable regulations. Each member state designates one. They can request technical documentation, inspect products, ban sales, order recalls, and issue fines. Under the CRA, they typically give you 30-60 days to respond to a documentation request.

placing_on_market.title: Placing on the market
placing_on_market.body: The legal moment at which a product is first made available on the EU market for distribution or use, whether in return for payment or free of charge. From that moment the manufacturer's CRA obligations begin, and the support period starts counting. Giving a device to a customer in the EU is placing on the market, even a beta unit.

early_warning.title: Early warning
early_warning.body: The first of three CRA incident notifications. Must reach ENISA within 24 hours of becoming aware of an actively-exploited vulnerability or severe incident. Minimum content: general description, whether you suspect malicious activity, and the EU member state(s) where the product is in use. Can be terse — the 72-hour report fills in the gaps.

incident_report.title: Incident report
incident_report.body: The 72-hour follow-up to the 24-hour early warning. Includes severity assessment, scope of the compromise, indicators of compromise (IOCs) where available, mitigation in progress, and affected products and users. Can still be revised in the 14-day final report.

final_report.title: Final report
final_report.body: The 14-day closing notification. Root-cause analysis, the corrective measures you've taken (and will take), residual risk, and how you communicated to affected users. This is the one auditors will dig into years later, so be thorough.

triage.title: Triage
triage.body: The process of deciding, for every incoming vulnerability or report, what severity it really is and what to do about it. In Seentrix, triage moves a report along the triage → investigating → resolved / duplicate / spam pipeline and attaches notes that become part of the audit trail.

severity.title: Severity
severity.body: A qualitative label (Critical / High / Medium / Low) for how serious a vulnerability or incident is in your specific context. Not the same as CVSS: a Critical CVSS score on a component you don't actually use is Low severity. Severity drives the CRA reporting obligations under Article 14.

enisa.title: ENISA
enisa.body: The European Union Agency for Cybersecurity. Under the CRA, ENISA operates the single reporting platform where manufacturers file the 24h / 72h / 14d incident notifications. Also maintains the European Vulnerability Database (EUVD) and coordinates with national CSIRTs.

csirt.title: CSIRT
csirt.body: Computer Security Incident Response Team — national-level teams (one per EU member state plus ENISA's own) that coordinate incident response. Under the CRA, CSIRTs receive Article 14 notifications from the single ENISA platform and may contact you directly for more information.

support_period.title: Support period
support_period.body: The window during which a manufacturer commits to providing security updates for a product. Declared publicly (on end-user information, the DoC, and product packaging) and binding — you can't shorten it after the fact. The CRA mandates a minimum of 5 years, longer if the expected product lifetime is longer.
support_period.ref: CRA Article 13 + Annex I Part II(8).

update_channel.title: Update channel
update_channel.body: How security updates physically reach end users — over-the-air push, package manager pull (apt, npm, Docker Hub), a download portal, a vendor-shipped USB update, or manual replacement. The CRA requires the update channel to be declared on end-user information so users know how to stay current.

release.title: Release
release.body: A single shippable version of a product, characterised by a version string and a build artifact. Seentrix tracks releases so you can evidence the support commitment is being met: a 5-year support period with zero releases is a red flag for auditors.

security_update.title: Security update
security_update.body: A release whose primary purpose is patching one or more vulnerabilities. Under CRA Article 13(8) security updates must be made available "without undue delay and free of charge." Tagging a release as a security update in Seentrix auto-flags it in the compliance evidence ledger.

annex_vii.title: Annex VII (technical documentation)
annex_vii.body: The CRA's checklist of what a product's technical file must contain: a general description and intended purpose; the design, development and production information — including architecture and data-flow diagrams (2(a)), the SBOM, coordinated-vulnerability-disclosure policy and secure-update description (2(b)); the cybersecurity risk assessment; the support-period justification; the standards applied; reports of the security tests carried out; and the Declaration of Conformity. Seentrix's Diagrams & Evidence tab feeds points 1.3, 2(a) and 6.
annex_vii.ref: CRA Annex VII — the technical documentation a manufacturer must draw up before placing a product on the market and keep for 10 years (or the support period, if longer).

threat_model.title: Threat model
threat_model.body: A structured analysis of how a product could be attacked: what assets it protects, the trust boundaries data crosses, the entry points an attacker could reach, and the mitigations in place for each plausible threat. Threat modelling (e.g. STRIDE over a data-flow diagram) is the expected secure-by-design practice behind a credible CRA risk assessment — it turns 'we thought about security' into a documented artifact an auditor can follow.
threat_model.ref: Threat modelling is not named verbatim in the CRA, but Annex I Part I(1) (secure-by-design) and the Article 13 risk assessment effectively require it.

data_flow_diagram.title: Data-flow diagram
data_flow_diagram.body: A drawing that shows how data moves through a product — the processes, data stores, external entities, and the trust boundaries between them. It is the canvas threat modelling is done on, and one of the diagrams CRA Annex VII 2(a) expects in the technical file alongside the system-architecture description.
data_flow_diagram.ref: CRA Annex VII 2(a) — the technical documentation must include a description of the design and development, including data-flow diagrams.

risk_assessment.title: Cybersecurity risk assessment
risk_assessment.body: The structured analysis CRA Article 13(2)-(3) requires before and throughout a product's life. It documents the intended purpose, operational environment, the assets the product protects and the expected lifetime, then maps every Annex I requirement to 'applies' (with the mitigating implementation and the residual risk) or 'not applicable' (with a written justification). It must be kept up to date and dated across the support period and forms point 3 of the Annex VII technical file.
risk_assessment.ref: CRA Article 13(2)-(3) and Annex VII point 3 — the risk assessment must be carried out, documented, taken into account during design and development, and kept up to date.

residual_risk.title: Residual risk
residual_risk.body: The level of risk that remains for a given threat once the mitigating controls are in place — as opposed to the inherent risk before mitigation. In the Seentrix risk assessment each applicable Annex I requirement records an inherent risk (derived from a Low/Medium/High likelihood × impact matrix) and the residual risk left after the implementation described is applied.
residual_risk.ref: Supports CRA Article 13(3): the risk assessment must show that identified risks are mitigated to an acceptable level.

technical_documentation.title: Technical documentation (Annex VII)
technical_documentation.body: The set of documents CRA Annex VII requires a manufacturer to compile and keep current: a general product description, the design and development (system architecture and data-flow diagrams, the SBOM and vulnerability-handling processes, production and monitoring), the risk assessment, the support period, the standards applied, the test reports, and the Declaration of Conformity. It must exist before the product is placed on the market. In Seentrix the Technical File tab assembles it in Annex VII order from the other tabs.
technical_documentation.ref: CRA Annex VII and Article 13 — the technical documentation must be drawn up before placing on the market and kept up to date.

retention_period.title: Retention period
retention_period.body: How long the CRA requires compliance records to be kept available to authorities. The technical documentation and the EU Declaration of Conformity must be retained for at least ten years after the product is placed on the market, or for the support period if that is longer. Seentrix stamps this deadline on each released technical file and keeps released files (soft-archived) rather than deleting them.
retention_period.ref: CRA Article 13(13): keep the technical documentation and DoC available to market-surveillance authorities for at least 10 years, or the support period if longer.

single_reporting_platform.title: Single Reporting Platform (SRP)
single_reporting_platform.body: The single electronic entry point established by ENISA for filing CRA Article 14 notifications. The manufacturer submits the 24-hour early warning, the 72-hour intermediate report and the final report through it; the platform routes them to the coordinating CSIRT and the relevant authorities and returns a reference number per submission. In Seentrix each incident exports a structured submission package per stage and records the SRP reference number it returns.
single_reporting_platform.ref: CRA Article 14: severe incidents and actively-exploited vulnerabilities are notified via the single reporting platform established by ENISA.

annex_ii.title: Annex II — User information
annex_ii.body: The information and instructions to the user that must accompany a product with digital elements: the manufacturer's identity and single point of contact, where to find the vulnerability-disclosure policy, the intended use and known/foreseeable cybersecurity risks, the support-period end-date, where the EU Declaration of Conformity can be accessed, and secure installation/operation/decommissioning guidance. In Seentrix it is generated as the end-user information sheet from the Identity & CE tab.
annex_ii.ref: CRA Annex II — information and instructions to the user; the support-period end-date is buyer-facing under Article 13(19).

annex_vi.title: Annex VI — Simplified DoC
annex_vi.body: The simplified EU declaration of conformity: a short form naming the product and manufacturer plus a statement that the full declaration of conformity is available at a stated internet address. It is what a manufacturer can print on the product or packaging; the address points to a public page hosting the declaration. Seentrix publishes it at /doc/<org-slug>/<product-id> when the product is published and the org has public pages enabled.
annex_vi.ref: CRA Annex VI — simplified EU declaration of conformity.

hero.title: Seentrix Academy
hero.description: Short lessons on the CRA, vulnerability handling, SBOMs, and the obligations behind every field you fill in across Seentrix. Complete tracks to collect audit-ready certificates for each team member.

byScreenCard.meta: {count, plural, one {# lesson} other {# lessons}} · {minutes} min · {done}/{total} done

quiz.passedBody: You scored {score}%. Completion recorded to your audit trail.
quiz.failedBody: Review the feedback above, then retry in {minutes} min.
quiz.cooldownBody: You can retry in {minutes} min. The delay is there to make sure the quiz is actually teaching the concept.
quiz.alreadyPassedShort: You have completed this lesson. It is locked to preserve your training record.

teamProgress.heading: Team training status
teamProgress.description: Who has passed which required lesson, when, and at what score. Audit-ready — download a CSV for regulators.

banner.title: New to this screen?
banner.body: {count, plural, one {# lesson covers it in {minutes} min} other {# lessons cover it in {minutes} min}}.

gate.title: Complete your training to continue
gate.subtitle: Every team member must pass the required lessons for their role before using Seentrix. Mandatory under CRA and our organisation's audit policy.

lesson.disclaimer: Educational material, not legal advice. Consult a qualified CRA expert for legally binding decisions.
lesson.quizIntro: Five multiple-choice questions. Pass 4 of 5 to mark this lesson complete.
lesson.downloadCertificateHint: Download your completion certificate as a PDF.

verify.title: Verify a certificate
verify.description: Paste a certificate number to confirm it is genuine and see which team member it belongs to. Lookups are limited to your organisation.
verify.notFound: No certificate matches that number in your organisation. It may be mistyped or invalid.
verify.forbidden: Only admins and compliance officers can verify certificates.
verify.error: Something went wrong while verifying. Please try again.
